---
title: "Day 22 — Bigram baseline to one head of masked self-attention"
subtitle: "Friday, 14 August 2026 · Week 4 · Build a GPT · Study day · 3-4 h"
---

::: {.day-nav .kind-study}
[← Day 21](day-21.qmd) · [All 50 days](../timeline.qmd) · [Day 23 →](day-23.qmd)
:::

::: {.why}
Three weeks of setup come down to one idea today: a token computing a weighted average of the tokens before it, with weights it produces itself from the content of those tokens.
:::

## What you should be able to do tonight

- You can write the masked attention weight matrix for a 4-token sequence by hand, before and after softmax, and say what each row sums to and why the first row has only one non-zero entry.
- You can explain why the scores are multiplied by head_size**-0.5, in terms of the variance of a dot product between two vectors of length head_size with unit-variance entries.
- You can say what breaks if you delete the tril mask, and why an encoder for translation is allowed to do exactly that.

## Tasks <span class='task-total'>210 min</span>

::: {.task-list}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [55 min]{.task-min} Watch kCc8FmEb1nY from 00:00:00 to 00:38:00 and type the bigram baseline into a fresh `gpt.py` yourself: reading input.txt, the character vocab (65 symbols), encode/decode, the 90/10 train-val split, `get_batch`, `BigramLanguageModel` with `forward` and `generate`, and the AdamW loop. Do not paste from the repo.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [45 min]{.task-min} Work through 00:42:13 to 01:00:00, the three ways to average the past: the explicit double for-loop, `torch.tril` normalized so rows sum to 1 and applied by matmul, then `masked_fill(tril == 0, float('-inf'))` followed by softmax. Print `wei` at each version for T=8 and assert that all three produce the same `xbow` tensor.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [55 min]{.task-min} Watch 01:00:18 to 01:19:11 and implement a single `Head`: key, query and value projections with `bias=False`, the masked scaled dot product, and the position embedding table added to the token embedding before the block. Karpathy's six notes at the end of that stretch are the conceptual payload, not filler.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [35 min]{.task-min} Derive the sqrt(head_size) scaling on paper first: with q and k entries iid at unit variance, Var(q . k) = head_size, so unscaled scores grow with head_size and softmax collapses toward one-hot. Then check it numerically at head_size=64 with random q and k, printing the variance of the scores and the max of the softmax output with and without the scale.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [20 min]{.task-min} Stop at the end of the single-head model, before multi-head. Write three sentences in `notes.md` naming something a bigram model structurally cannot do that one attention head can, using an actual line of Shakespeare as the example.
:::
:::

## Resources

- [Karpathy L7 - Let's build GPT from scratch (1h56m)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kCc8FmEb1nY) [video]{.res-kind}
- [nn-zero-to-hero repo](https://github.com/karpathy/nn-zero-to-hero) [repo]{.res-kind}
- [SLP3 Ch8 - Transformers](https://web.stanford.edu/~jurafsky/slp3/8.pdf) [textbook]{.res-kind}
- [Attention Is All You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762) [paper]{.res-kind}

## Deliverable

::: {.deliverable}
`gpt.py` containing a trained bigram baseline (val loss around 2.5) and a single-head attention model that beats it, with both losses written down, plus the three-version equivalence check on `wei` and the sqrt(head_size) variance experiment.
:::

## Checkpoint

::: {.checkpoint}
Without looking: what does `wei` look like at initialization if you delete `* head_size**-0.5`, and what does that do to the gradient flowing back through the softmax?
:::

## Prepare for tomorrow

Ten minutes tonight so Day 23, Saturday, 15 August 2026 starts clean. This is what the evening email sends you.

::: {.task-list .prep}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Run `gpt.py` for 200 steps once more before you close the laptop, so tomorrow starts from a file you know works.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Queue kCc8FmEb1nY at 01:19:11 in a pinned tab so tomorrow starts exactly where you stopped.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Check `torch.cuda.is_available()`. If it is False, open Colab and confirm you can get a T4, because tomorrow's scaled-up model is painful on CPU.
:::
:::
